deck: Recorded at the Global Digital Health Forum 2022, Jonathan Jackson sits down with FIND's Olukunle Akinwusi and Jhpiego's Erica Troncoso on closing diagnostic gaps, designing with frontline workers, and earning country buy-in for digital health.
seo_description: FIND's Olukunle Akinwusi and Jhpiego's Erica Troncoso on diagnostics, user-centered design, and country buy-in, recorded at the Global Digital Health Forum 2022.
og_description: FIND and Jhpiego on diagnostics, designing with frontline workers, and country buy-in, from the Global Digital Health Forum 2022.
about: Recorded at the Global Digital Health Forum 2022, this episode brings Jonathan Jackson together with Olukunle Akinwusi, Technical Officer for Digital Access at FIND, the Global Alliance for Diagnostics, and Erica Troncoso, Technical Advisor and Portfolio Lead for Frontier Technology Solutions at Jhpiego. The conversation centers on how thoughtful diagnostics and digital tools can improve health equity in low and middle income countries.

Akinwusi traces why diagnostics are pivotal to the cascade of care, since people cannot be linked to treatment before they are identified and diagnosed. He describes FIND's work with ministries of health, including adapting rapid antigen testing for decentralized COVID-19 response in Rwanda, and the data cycle of using insights from deployments to inform what to design next.

The discussion returns repeatedly to designing with the end user. Akinwusi explains FIND's reliance on the principles of digital development, the surprises that emerge when health workers repurpose tools in ways the designers never planned, and his guidance for practitioners: engage the country, design alongside the real workflow, and secure genuine buy-in before anything is built.

pull_quote.text: It's important that a workflow of the context in which the solution will be used is taken into consideration, because if a solution is designed or developed and it does not align with the workflow where it's gonna be deployed, then the adoption is unlikely.
pull_quote.cite: Olukunle Akinwusi, on designing for the real workflow

bullets: Diagnostics first: why no one can be linked to treatment before they are identified and diagnosed, and where the biggest diagnostic gaps sit across the cascade of care
Decentralizing testing: how FIND worked with the Ministry of Health in Rwanda to adapt rapid antigen testing for improved COVID-19 response
A data cycle: using deployment insights to inform what to design next, then feeding new data back into the response
Build with the users: FIND's reliance on the principles of digital development to keep solutions aligned to the frontline worker's real workflow
Aha moments: when health workers repurposed a patient-monitoring tablet to set their own follow-up reminders, revealing a need no one had designed for
Three pivotal steps: engage the country, design alongside the workflow, and secure genuine buy-in before a product is built
Defining buy-in: why stakeholders, governments, and partners must agree on what buy-in actually means and how to measure it

stats.label: the HIV care cascade target: 90% know their status, 90% of those are on ART, and 90% of those are virally suppressed

guests.name: Olukunle Akinwusi
guests.role: Technical Officer for Digital Access, a computer scientist designing and deploying digital health interventions to improve access to diagnostics in low and middle income countries.
guests.org: FIND, the Global Alliance for Diagnostics

guests.name: Erica Troncoso
guests.role: Technical Advisor and Portfolio Lead for Frontier Technology Solutions, working on digital innovation to advance health equity.
